message.content:
{
  "homework": {
    "sessionInformation": {
      "sessionNumber": "1",
      "sessionTitle": "Number System",
      "subject": "Mathematics",
      "grade": "Class IX / Class X",
      "difficultyLevel": "Moderate",
      "learningPace": "Balanced",
      "estimatedHomeworkDuration": "45 minutes"
    },
    "homework": [
      {
        "id": 1,
        "type": "MCQs",
        "title": "Conceptual Recall: Rational and Irrational Numbers",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Identify rational vs irrational numbers"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Rational Numbers", "Irrational Numbers", "Definitions"],
        "difficulty": "Easy",
        "marks": 4,
        "estimatedTime": "8 minutes",
        "instructions": "Select the correct option for each question based on the definitions and properties discussed in class.",
        "question": "Which of the following statements is true regarding rational numbers?",
        "options": [
          "A. Every integer is a rational number but not every rational number is an integer.",
          "B. The square root of any prime number is always a rational number.",
          "C. A terminating decimal cannot be expressed as a fraction p/q where q ≠ 0.",
          "D. All irrational numbers are integers."
        ],
        "answerSpace": "Circle the correct letter (A, B, C, or D).",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Option A"
      },
      {
        "id": 2,
        "type": "Numerical Problem Solving",
        "title": "Decimal Expansions and Classification",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Classify numbers based on decimal expansion"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Terminating Decimals", "Non-terminating Recurring Decimals", "Classification of Real Numbers"],
        "difficulty": "Moderate",
        "marks": 6,
        "estimatedTime": "12 minutes",
        "instructions": "Perform the division or analyze the given numbers to determine their nature. State whether each is rational (terminating) or irrational.",
        "question": "Analyze the following real numbers:\n(a) $\\frac{3}{8}$\n(b) $0.125$\n(c) $\\sqrt{7}$\n(d) $\\frac{4}{9}$\n\nFor each, determine if it is a terminating decimal or non-terminating recurring decimal (if rational), and classify as Rational or Irrational.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Write your classification for each number below the question.",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "(a) Terminating, Rational; (b) Terminating, Rational; (c) Non-terminating non-recurring, Irrational; (d) Recurring, Rational"
      },
      {
        "id": 3,
        "type": "Application & Reasoning",
        "title": "Real-world Application: Number Line Representation",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Represent numbers on a number line"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Number Line", "Locating Irrational Numbers (Conceptual)"],
        "difficulty": "Moderate",
        "marks": 5,
        "estimatedTime": "10 minutes",
        "instructions": "Describe the process of locating $\\sqrt{2}$ on a number line using geometric construction principles discussed in class. You do not need to draw it perfectly but describe the steps.",
        "question": "Explain how you would locate the point representing $\\sqrt{2}$ on a number line starting from 0 and 1, assuming you have a ruler and compass (or can visualize the geometric construction). What is the length of the hypotenuse used in this process?",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Write your explanation.",
        "visualRequirement": "Describe the right-angled triangle with legs of unit length 1 that forms the basis for finding $\\sqrt{2}$.",
        "expectedResponse": "Construct a right-angled triangle with both perpendicular sides (legs) equal to 1 unit. By Pythagoras theorem, hypotenuse = $\\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \\sqrt{2}$. Transfer this length from the origin on the number line."
      },
      {
        "id": 4,
        "type": "Short Answer / Analysis",
        "title": "Properties of Real Numbers: Rationalizing and Operations",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Apply properties of real numbers"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Operations on Real Numbers", "Rationalization (Conceptual)"],
        "difficulty": "Moderate to Hard",
        "marks": 5,
        "estimatedTime": "10 minutes",
        "instructions": "Answer the following conceptual questions regarding operations between rational and irrational numbers.",
        "question": "(a) Is the sum of a rational number and an irrational number always irrational? Justify with one example.\n(b) Can the product of two irrational numbers be a rational number? Give an example to support your answer.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Write 'Yes' or 'No' followed by justification/example for each part.",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "(a) Yes. Example: $2 + \\sqrt{3}$ is irrational.\n(b) Yes. Example: $\\sqrt{2} \\times \\sqrt{8} = \\sqrt{16} = 4$ (which is rational)."
      },
      {
        "id": 5,
        "type": "Differentiation / Extension",
        "title": "Challenge Question: HOTS on Number Systems",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Higher Order Thinking"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Complex Properties of Real Numbers"],
        "difficulty": "Hard",
        "marks": 5,
        "estimatedTime": "10 minutes",
        "instructions": "This question is for students who finish early or seek a challenge. Analyze the relationship between density and ordering.",
        "question": "Between any two distinct rational numbers, there exists an infinite number of other rational numbers (Density Property). Does this property hold true if we replace 'rational' with 'irrational'? Explain your reasoning briefly without using complex proofs.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Write a short paragraph explaining the density of irrational numbers between two rationals or irrationals.",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Yes, the property holds. Between any two distinct real numbers (rational or irrational), there are infinitely many rational and infinitely many irrational numbers."
      }
    ],
    "summary": {
      "totalQuestions": 5,
      "totalMarks": 25,
      "estimatedCompletionTime": "40-45 minutes",
      "learningOutcomesCovered": [
        "Identify rational vs irrational numbers",
        "Classify numbers based on decimal expansion",
        "Represent numbers on a number line",
        "Apply properties of real numbers"
      ],
      "topicsCovered": [
        "Rational Numbers",
        "Irrational Numbers",
        "Terminating Decimals",
        "Number Line Representation",
        "Operations on Real Numbers"
      ],
      "subtopicsCovered": [
        "Definitions and Properties",
        "Classification of Reals",
        "Geometric Construction for $\\sqrt{2}$",
        "Sum/Product properties"
      ],
      "taskDistribution": {
        "MCQs": 1,
        "Numerical Problems": 1,
        "Application & Reasoning": 1,
        "Short Answer / Analysis": 1,
        "Challenge Question": 1
      },
      "homeExperimentIncluded": false,
      "parentEngagementIncluded": true
    }
  }
}
